data.reasoningText:
The issue is clear now. The `@ai-sdk/svelte` package has an `exports` field that uses the `"svelte"` condition, but there's no `"import"` or `"default"` condition. TypeScript's language server (used by VS Code) resolves modules using specific conditions, and by default it uses `"types"`, `"import"`, `"default"`, etc. The `"svelte"` condition isn't a standard TypeScript condition, so TypeScript doesn't know how to resolve the package.

However, the app compiles fine because Vite/SvelteKit understands the `"svelte"` export condition.
I can fix this by adding `"customConditions": ["svelte"]` to the `compilerOptions` in `tsconfig.json` to tell TypeScript to recognize that export condition. Let me look at what's currently in the config.

data.reasoningText:
The issue is clear. The `@ai-sdk/svelte` package exports only use the `"svelte"` condition (no `"import"` or `"default"` condition), so TypeScript's language server can't resolve it. Vite/SvelteKit handles this fine because it resolves the `"svelte"` export condition.

The fix is to add `"customConditions": ["svelte"]` to `compilerOptions` in `tsconfig.json`. This tells TypeScript to also consider the `"svelte"` export condition when resolving modules.

Wait, I need to check what TypeScript version is being used and whether `customConditions` is supported. It was added in TypeScript 5.x, so it should be fine.
Actually, I see `"moduleResolution": "bundler"` is already set in the tsconfig, which should support custom conditions. Let me check the SvelteKit-generated tsconfig to see if `customConditions` is already configured there.

data.content:
El problema es que `@ai-sdk/svelte` solo declara la condición de exportación `"svelte"` (sin `"import"` ni `"default"`). Vite la resuelve sin problemas, pero el servidor de lenguaje de TypeScript en VS Code no conoce esa condición por defecto.

La solución es agregar `"customConditions": ["svelte"]` al `tsconfig.json`:
data.reasoningText:
The issue is that `@ai-sdk/svelte` only exposes the `"svelte"` export condition but not `"import"` or `"default"`. TypeScript's language server, even with `moduleResolution: "bundler"`, doesn't automatically understand the `"svelte"` condition. 

The fix is to add `"customConditions": ["svelte"]` to `compilerOptions` in `tsconfig.json`. This was introduced in TypeScript 5.2.

Let me add it.

data.content: Con `"customConditions": ["svelte"]` el TS language server de VS Code también resuelve la condición de exportación `"svelte"`, igual que lo hace Vite. El error debería desaparecer al guardar/recargar el proyecto (puede que necesites esperar unos segundos a que `svelte-check` reinicie).

data.content: Ahora necesito ver cómo están implementados los componentes `MessageScroller`:
data.reasoningText:
Now I understand the layout structure. The problems are:

1. The `Card.Root` has `h-fit max-h-full` which means it tries to be as tall as its content but limited by the parent. But the parent `div.relative.flex.flex-col.gap-4` doesn't have a constrained height, so it will just grow to fit all content.

2. `Card.Content class="flex-1 overflow-hidden p-0"` — `flex-1` only works inside a flex container with a fixed height. If the card is `h-fit`, the content area will just expand to fit, and there's nothing to scroll.
3. The `MessageScroller.Root` can't determine its viewport size because the card above it doesn't have a fixed height either. The real issue is that the Card wrapper is the wrong abstraction here — I need to restructure this as a proper flex column that fills available space, with the header and separator shrinking to their content size while the message scroller takes up the remaining height.

The solution is to use `flex-1 min-h-0` on the scroller container, which forces it to take up available vertical space while allowing its children to scroll properly. The layout becomes: outer flex column with full height, a fixed-height header, separator, then the scroller area with flex-1 and min-h-0, and finally a shrink-0 footer for the input.

Rewriting the template structure...

Now I understand the full layout. The `MessageScroller.Root` (`message-scroller.svelte`) renders:
```
<div class="group/message-scroller relative flex size-full min-h-0 flex-col overflow-hidden" ...>
```

And the `MessageScroller.Viewport` renders:
```
<div class="size-full min-h-0 min-w-0 overflow-y-auto overscroll-contain contain-content" ...>
```

Both use `size-full` (w-full h-full). So they need their parent to have a defined height.

The problem in the current code:
The outer panel div with `flex h-full flex-col` is set up correctly, but after the Separator, the `MessageScroller.Provider` wraps a div with `relative flex flex-col gap-4` that has no height constraint, so it expands to fit its content instead of maintaining a fixed height. The `Card.Root` inside uses `h-fit max-h-full`, but since the parent has no defined height, `max-h-full` resolves to `auto`. The `Card.Content` has `flex-1 overflow-hidden`, which should make it grow to fill available space, but there's no available space to fill because the parent container isn't constrained.

The solution is to remove the Card wrapper entirely and restructure with a clean flex column layout where the message area gets `flex-1 min-h-0` to properly fill the viewport and enable scrolling.

Restructuring the layout... Finishing the layout structure... Writing the header layout... Building the message display with an empty state that shows a greeting, then rendering the chat messages in a scrollable container with animations for each message.

Now I'm setting up the input form at the bottom with a text preview that shows what the user is about to send, handling form submission to send messages when ready. Writing dropdown menu items... In a flex container, `flex-1` does give the child a computed height through the flex algorithm, so `h-full` on `MessageScroller.Root` should work with its `size-full` and `min-h-0` classes. The real issue is that the conditional rendering switches between `Empty.Root` and `MessageScroller.Root` depending on whether there are messages, but both need to respect the parent's flex layout properly. I need to trace through the component hierarchy to understand how the height is being computed. The outer panel has `h-full`, and the `MessageScroller.Provider` is nested inside — I should check what that provider actually renders to see if it's preserving the flex layout properly.

`MessageScroller.Provider` renders no wrapper element — it just provides context. So the structure is:

- `div.flex.h-full.flex-col.bg-background` (outer panel)
  - `div.flex.h-9.shrink-0...` (header)
  - `Separator` (separator)
  - [Provider renders nothing]
  - `div.relative.flex.flex-col.gap-4` (current problematic div)
    - `Card.Root` (h-fit max-h-full)

The Provider's children are the `div.relative.flex.flex-col.gap-4` div and the closing `</MessageScroller.Provider>` is after that.
Looking back at the actual markup, I can see the Card is nested inside that relative flex div with the gap spacing, and it's constrained with max-height and width limits.

The fix is to restructure this so the MessageScroller.Provider wraps a flex container that properly fills the remaining vertical space, then conditionally render either an empty state or the message card depending on whether there are any messages. child with `h-full` should inherit the parent's computed height in a flex layout, but the cascade depends on the outer container having an explicit height set. I need to trace through the hierarchy to see where the height is actually being constrained.

data.content: Entendido el problema. El `Card.Root` con `h-fit` hace que el área de mensajes crezca sin límite en lugar de ocupar el espacio disponible — el `MessageScroller` no tiene altura acotada para hacer scroll. Necesito eliminar el `Card` wrapper y usar un layout flex limpio.

Good. So the `AIChatPanel` is inside a `PaneForge` (which is `Resizable.Pane`), and `PaneForge` likely has `h-full` on it (since it's a resizable pane). So the panel itself should have `h-full` to fill the pane.

The outer div of the panel has `flex h-full flex-col bg-background` which is correct.

Now for the fix, the main problem is:
1. The `MessageScroller.Provider` renders its children directly (no wrapper div)
2. The direct child is `div.relative.flex.flex-col.gap-4` — this div is a flex child of `div.flex.h-full.flex-col.bg-background`, but it has NO `flex-1` on it, so it doesn't stretch. Instead, it just wraps its content.
3. The `Card.Root` has `h-fit max-h-full` which means it collapses to content height, and the `Card.Content` has `flex-1 overflow-hidden` but that's meaningless inside an `h-fit` container.

The solution is to restructure the flex container to use `min-h-0` and `flex-1` on the middle div, remove the restrictive Card wrapper, and let the `MessageScroller.Root` handle the scrolling properly.

Now I'm thinking through whether `h-full` on the child actually works when the parent has `flex-1` — the parent gets a computed height from the flex context, so the child's `h-full` should resolve to that computed height, but I need to verify the parent is actually establishing a height constraint. I'll simplify by using `flex-1 min-h-0` directly on the MessageScroller instead of relying on `size-full`, then restructure the template by removing the Card wrappers, adjusting the flex layout, and repositioning the reset button and input form into the header area. also need `flex-1` on the `Empty.Root` component to match the space-filling behavior. The tricky part is that `MessageScroller.Root` already has `h-full` from its base classes, and adding `flex-1` might create a conflict since both are trying to control height — though in a flex context, `flex-1` should take precedence for the grow behavior.

Let me write out the complete replacement with the header and layout structure.

data.content:
Sin errores. El layout final quedó así:

```
div.flex.h-full.flex-col          ← ocupa todo el panel
  ├─ header (shrink-0)            ← fijo, con botón reset + cerrar
  ├─ Separator (shrink-0)
  └─ MessageScroller.Provider
       └─ div.flex.min-h-0.flex-1.flex-col   ← ← LA CLAVE: min-h-0 permite que flex-1 achique
            ├─ MessageScroller.Root.flex-1   ← crece y hace scroll internamente
            │    ├─ Viewport (overflow-y-auto)
            │    └─ Button (scroll-to-bottom)
            └─ div.shrink-0.border-t        ← input siempre visible abajo
```

El problema era que `Card.Root` tenía `h-fit` (crecía con el contenido) y el `div` contenedor no tenía `flex-1 min-h-0`, por lo que el `MessageScroller.Viewport` nunca tenía una altura acotada para activar `overflow-y-auto`. Con `flex-1 min-h-0` en el contenedor y `flex-1` en `MessageScroller.Root`, la cadena de alturas se resuelve correctamente.

", "oldString": "	const chat = createChat()
		.user(
			"I'm building a chat for our app and the scroll behavior is driving me nuts. Every time the AI streams a reply, the whole thread jumps around."
		)
		.sleep(1000)
		.assistant(
			"That's the classic streaming scroll problem. Wrap your message list in `MessageScroller` and turn on `autoScroll` — the viewport pins to the bottom as tokens arrive, so users always see the latest text land in place.\n\nThe important part: it only auto-scrolls while the reader is already at the bottom. The moment they scroll up to read something earlier, auto-scroll backs off and their position is preserved. You get smooth streaming without fighting the user's intent."
		)
		.user(
			"Okay, but when someone sends a new message the view still feels jarring — like the whole conversation reloads from the top."
		)
		.sleep(1000)
		.assistant(
			"MessageScrollerItem fixes that with turn anchoring. Set `scrollAnchor` on the turn that should settle near the top instead of blindly snapping to the document bottom.\n\nIt also leaves a small peek of the previous exchange visible above the anchor, so context isn't lost. The reply starts in view without that disorienting jump you get from a plain overflow container."
		)
		.user(
			"And if they've scrolled up to re-read an older answer? I don't want to yank them back down."
		)
		.sleep(1000)
		.assistant(
			"You won't. Auto-scroll only runs when the viewport is already pinned to the bottom, so scrolling up is a deliberate opt-out — their place in the thread stays put even as new tokens keep arriving below.\n\nWhen there is content they haven't seen yet, `MessageScrollerButton` appears at the bottom of the viewport. One tap jumps them back to the newest message and re-engages auto-scroll. Same pattern as Slack or iMessage: quiet when you're caught up, helpful when you're not."
		)
		.user("Last one — does this work with assistive tech?")
		.sleep(1000)
		.assistant(
			'`MessageScrollerContent` sets `role="log"` and `aria-relevant="additions"` by default, so screen readers announce new messages as they stream in.\n\nThe scroll button is a real `<button>` with an sr-only label, and it\'s removed from the tab order when you\'re already at the bottom — no ghost focus stops.'
		);
"}

data.content: Listo. Los mensajes ahora simulan una consulta real a un ERP inteligente: el asistente detecta proactivamente alertas de inventario, recomienda proveedores con justificación, advierte de una factura vencida que bloquea una orden, anticipa el impacto de un proyecto (OT-445) antes de que el usuario lo mencione, ajusta cantidades, aplica descuentos automáticos y escala el flujo de autorización — todo sin que el usuario tenga que pedirlo explícitamente.
